title: Pyrography geometric pattern
subtitle: A chevron border and central motif burned on a basswood panel
excerpt: A geometric pyrography panel on basswood: a chevron border around the perimeter and a simple eight-pointed star at the centre. Geometric patterns are the ideal introduction to pyrography; they teach consistent line weight and temperature control without requiring freehand drawing skill. The design is marked out in pencil with ruler and compass before burning.
sourceNotes: Central and Eastern European geometric pyrography tradition; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for compass-and-rule design layout on wood panels.

content.text: Work in a well-ventilated room; burning wood produces light smoke. Geometric
pyrography

has one advantage over freehand botanical work: every line is pre-marked with ruler and compass, and the burner simply follows the pencil line. The difficulty is maintaining consistent pressure and speed so that every line in the border is the same weight. A solid-tip burner at medium temperature is the right tool for this work; the fixed temperature means line weight is controlled by speed alone.
The panel

A basswood (lime) panel: 20 by 20 cm, 6 mm thick, sanded to 240 grit. Basswood is the softest commercial carving and pyrography wood; it burns at lower temperatures and is more forgiving of technique variation than sycamore or beech. Birch plywood (sanded to 240 grit) is an alternative; it is harder but has a very even grain that takes geometric patterns well.
Design layout

Mark out the full design in 2H pencil before burning. The border: a row of chevrons 8 mm wide, each chevron 8 mm tall, running around the inner perimeter 15 mm from the panel edge. Use a ruler and square for all lines; any deviation from a straight line is visible in the finished burn. The central star: a circle 8 cm in diameter at the panel centre, divided into 8 equal sectors with a compass and protractor. Alternate sectors are filled with solid burning; the others are left pale, creating the eight-pointed star.
Method
Use the
writing tip

throughout. A solid-tip burner at medium temperature (the tip glows faintly orange but not bright red) produces a warm brown line in basswood. Test the temperature on the back of the panel before starting the design.

Burn the border outline first: run the writing tip along the outer boundary line of the chevron border at a consistent speed. Do not stop mid-line. Work the outer boundary, then the inner boundary, then the diagonal chevron lines between them. All lines should be the same weight.

Fill the alternating chevrons with solid burning: hold the tip flat against the wood surface inside the chevron outline and move slowly, overlapping each pass by 50 percent. Work in parallel passes along the chevron direction. The filled chevrons should be uniformly dark brown, with no unburned streaks visible between passes.

Burn the star sector outlines: run the writing tip along each radius and around the boundary circle. Alternate sectors are filled with solid burning using the same parallel-pass technique as the chevrons.
Add a light background
burnish

to the pale areas of the star if the contrast between filled and unfilled sectors needs strengthening. Move the writing tip very quickly across the surface of the pale sectors to lay a very light, uniform tone that unifies the background.
Finishing

Leave unfinished, or apply one coat of Danish oil after the burned marks have cooled fully. Danish oil slightly deepens the tone of the pale basswood background and reduces the contrast slightly; check on a test piece before applying to the finished panel.
Variations
Triangular fill pattern: swap the chevron border for a row of equal triangles. Sit the base against the panel edge. Fill in every other triangle and leave the rest blank.
Celtic knotwork: the same line-following technique applies; the complexity is in the layout, not the burning. Mark the full knotwork in pencil before burning any lines.
Care
Dust with a dry cloth. Hang away from direct moisture. The burned marks are permanent; the carbon produced by burning is stable and does not fade.

items.fix: Always allow the burner to reach full temperature before starting (3 to 5 minutes). Test line weight on the back of the panel. Move at a constant speed; use a sweeping arm motion rather than a hand movement.
items.cause: Inconsistent movement speed, or temperature varying as the tip heats up from a cold start
items.symptom: Lines vary in weight across the border; some dark, some pale

items.fix: Overlap each pass by at least 50 percent of the tip width. Work in parallel passes all in the same direction; do not cross-hatch.
items.symptom: Filled sectors show unburned streaks between passes

items.fix: Erase pencil marks lightly with a soft eraser after burning is complete. Do not sand; sanding removes the surface of the unburned wood and changes the tone.
items.cause: Pencil lines outside the burned marks, not removed before finishing
items.symptom: Pencil layout visible beside the burn lines after burning
